phrasalVerbs.definition: If separate amounts add up to a total amount, together they form that total.
examples: The bills add up to over £500 this month.
All the small expenses add up to a lot of money.
The scores add up to 100 points.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To give someone the same amount of money that you borrowed from them.
examples: I'll pay you back next week when I get paid.
She paid back the loan in six months.
Don't forget to pay back the money you owe me.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To remove one number or quantity from another number or quantity; to subtract.
examples: If you take away three from ten, you get seven.
Take away the discount and the price is still high.
Can you take away these old files from the desk?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To turn a container upside down so that the things inside it come out.
examples: She tipped up the box and everything fell out.
Tip up the bottle to get the last drops out.
He tipped up the bag to find his keys.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To use all of a supply of something.
examples: We've used up all the milk. Can you buy more?
She used up her holiday days by March.
Don't use up all the hot water!

fillInExercises.explanation: 'Add up to' means separate amounts form a total.

fillInExercises.sentence: I promise to ___ you back next Friday.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pay back' means to return borrowed money.
'Take away' means to subtract one number from another.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ up the bag to shake out the crumbs.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Tip up' means to turn something upside down to empty it.
'Use up' means to consume all of a resource.

fillInExercises.sentence: The total costs ___ up to more than we expected.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Add up to' means to reach a total when combined.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't worry, I'll ___ ___ the £20 I borrowed.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pay back' means to return money that was borrowed.

fillInExercises.sentence: Be careful not to ___ up all the paint.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Use up' means to finish all of something.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: My expenses this month [add up to|add up to] a lot more than usual.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: I know the feeling. Did you [pay back|pay back] the money you borrowed from Sarah?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Yes, I [paid back|pay back] her last week. But now I've [used up|use up] most of my savings.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: If you [take away|take away] the rent, how much do you have left?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Not much. I really need to budget better.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Maybe [tip up|tip up] your piggy bank and see if there's anything inside!

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Have we [used up|use up] all the office supplies already?

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Yes, the costs [add up to|add up to] quite a bit every month.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: We need to order more paper. Don't [use up|use up] the last pack.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I'll [tip up|tip up] the storage box and see what we have left.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Good idea. If you [take away|take away] what we don't need, we'll save money.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Also, remember the company will [pay back|pay back] any personal expenses.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Great! All my receipts [add up to|add up to] about £150 this quarter.
